findings.description: Clean three-package layering with no cycles. The dependency direction is strictly common <- ocr and common <- classifier, with zero cross-imports between ocr and classifier. Both domain packages import from common but never from each other. No shared mutable state or import-time side effects detected.
findings.evidence: grep for 'from classifier' in src/common/ and src/ocr/ returns empty; grep for 'from ocr' in src/common/ and src/classifier/ returns empty. All cross-package imports flow downward to common/.

findings.description: common/tags.py directly imports httpx for exception types used in error handling (httpx.HTTPError in get_latest_tags). This is a minor boundary leak: a helper module in common reaches past the PaperlessClient abstraction to reference the HTTP library's exception type directly. Ideally the PaperlessClient would either handle this internally or re-export/wrap the exception type.
findings.evidence: src/common/tags.py line 7: 'import httpx', used on line 41: 'except (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, ValueError)'

findings.description: openai SDK is imported directly in both domain provider modules (classifier/provider.py, ocr/provider.py) in addition to common/llm.py and common/preflight.py. This is acceptable since the providers are the actual consumers of the API, and common/llm.py provides a mixin rather than a complete facade. The boundary is consistent.
findings.evidence: openai imports appear in: common/llm.py, common/preflight.py, common/library_setup.py, classifier/provider.py, ocr/provider.py

findings.description: Naming conventions are highly consistent. All files use snake_case, all classes use PascalCase, all functions use snake_case. Both __init__.py files in domain packages follow the same pattern: docstring listing modules, selective re-exports, and __all__. common/__init__.py is empty (docstring only), which is a reasonable choice for the shared utilities package.
findings.evidence: Reviewed all 37 Python files. No camelCase functions or inconsistent naming found. Both classifier/__init__.py and ocr/__init__.py follow identical structural patterns.

findings.description: Both daemon entry points (ocr/daemon.py and classifier/daemon.py) follow an identical structural pattern: private _process_document helper, private _iter_docs iterator, and public main() function. This is good consistency. Minor note: the classifier daemon has a _process_document function that creates its own PaperlessClient per call, same as the OCR daemon, keeping the pattern symmetric.
findings.evidence: ocr/daemon.py defines _process_document, _iter_docs_to_ocr, main; classifier/daemon.py defines _process_document, _iter_docs_to_classify, main

findings.description: All 5 declared dependencies (httpx, openai, Pillow, pdf2image, structlog) are actively used in the codebase. No duplicate libraries for the same purpose. httpx is the sole HTTP client, openai is the sole LLM SDK, structlog is the sole logging framework. Version pinning uses compatible-release (~=) which is good practice.
findings.evidence: pyproject.toml declares 5 deps. grep confirms all are imported: httpx (4 files), openai (5 files), Pillow (4 files), pdf2image (1 file), structlog (19 files).

findings.description: pdf2image is used in exactly one file (ocr/image_converter.py) for PDF-to-image conversion. This is a relatively heavy dependency that requires poppler system binaries. However, PDF conversion is a core requirement for an OCR daemon, so this is justified usage rather than a light-use concern.
findings.evidence: src/ocr/image_converter.py:8 'from pdf2image import convert_from_bytes' is the only usage

findings.description: Pillow is imported in common/library_setup.py purely for configuration (setting MAX_IMAGE_PIXELS), but the actual image processing happens only in ocr/. This means common/ has a runtime dependency on Pillow even though the classifier daemon technically never processes images. This is a minor coupling issue.
findings.evidence: src/common/library_setup.py:10 'from PIL import Image' — used to set Image.MAX_IMAGE_PIXELS

findings.description: The three-package layout (common, ocr, classifier) under src/ is clean and well-organized. Package sizes are proportional to responsibility: common (17 files, shared infrastructure), classifier (13 files, classification domain), ocr (7 files, OCR domain). The src/ layout with setuptools find follows Python best practice.
findings.evidence: File counts: common/ has 17 .py files (1676 lines), classifier/ has 13 .py files (1509 lines), ocr/ has 7 .py files (748 lines). Total 3933 lines.

findings.description: common/ has 17 files which is on the higher side, but each file has a clear, focused responsibility (config, paperless client, retry, shutdown, tags, etc.). The module could potentially benefit from sub-grouping (e.g., lifecycle/ for bootstrap+shutdown+preflight, or api/ for paperless+retry), but at this scale the flat layout remains navigable.
findings.evidence: common/ files: bootstrap.py, claims.py, concurrency.py, config.py, daemon_loop.py, document_iter.py, library_setup.py, llm.py, logging_config.py, paperless.py, preflight.py, retry.py, shutdown.py, stale_lock.py, tags.py, utils.py

findings.description: The classifier package has well-separated concerns with dedicated modules for constants, normalizers, content_prep, quality_gates, tag_filters, metadata, taxonomy, result, prompts, provider, worker, and daemon. This is good domain-driven decomposition, though the number of small modules (normalizers at 58 lines, quality_gates at 29 lines, constants at 53 lines) suggests they could potentially be consolidated.
findings.evidence: classifier/ has 13 files. Several are quite small: quality_gates.py (29 lines), constants.py (53 lines), normalizers.py (58 lines)
